word_list.word: a good name is (far|much|way|) better than riches
word_list.pronunciation: …ź …°ňą äd nňąe…™m …™z fňą…Ďňź…Ļ mňą Ćt É wňąe…™ bňą…õ…ĺ…ö √į…źn …Ļňą…™t ÉŠĶĽz

senses.definition: used to imply that having a good reputation, character, and integrity is more important than having wealth or material possessions

examples.x: The business owner prioritized ethical practices and customer satisfaction over profit, believing that a good name is better than riches.

word_list.word: a good name (keeps|shall keep) its luster in the dark
word_list.pronunciation: …ź …°ňą äd nňąe…™m kňąiňźps …™ts lňą Ćst…ö…Ļ …™n√į…ô dňą…Ďňź…Ļk

senses.definition: used to imply that a person's good reputation and character will remain intact even when they are not in the public eye or when they are facing difficult circumstances

examples.x: Even though the successful businessman was facing false accusations, a good name keeps its luster in the dark, and his reputation remained untarnished.

word_list.word: give a dog a bad name (and hang him|)

senses.definition: used to suggest that once someone's reputation is tarnished, it can be difficult to restore

word_list.word: he that has an ill name is half hanged
word_list.pronunciation: hiňź √į√¶t h…źz …źn ňą…™l nňąe…™m …™z hňą√¶f hňą√¶Ňčd

senses.definition: used to imply that a person with a negative reputation is more likely to be judged harshly or punished severely, even if they are innocent

examples.x: Despite being innocent of the crime, the suspect's prior convictions led to a harsher sentence, demonstrating how he that has an ill name is half hanged.

word_list.word: an ill wound is cured, not an ill name
word_list.pronunciation: …źn ňą…™l wňąuňźnd …™z kjňą ä…Ļd nňĆ…Ďňźt …źn ňą…™l nňąe…™m

senses.definition: used to imply that physical wounds can heal with time and treatment, whereas damage to one's reputation or good name can be more lasting and difficult to repair

word_list.word: more people know Tom Fool than Tom Fool knows
word_list.pronunciation: mňąoňź…Ļ pňąiňźp…ôl nňąo ä tňą…Ďňźm fňąuňźl √į…źn tňą…Ďňźm fňąuňźl nňąo äz

senses.definition: used to warn about the potential pitfalls of notoriety or fame, and encourage careful consideration of one's actions and how they might be perceived by others

examples.x: The politician was warned that more people know Tom Fool than Tom Fool knows, so he should be careful about making any controversial statements that could damage his reputation.

word_list.word: there is no such thing as bad publicity
word_list.pronunciation: √į…õ…Ļ …™z nňąo ä sňą Ćt É őłňą…™Ňč √¶z bňą√¶d p Ćblňą…™s…™…ĺi

senses.definition: used to suggest that any publicity or attention, even if negative, is ultimately beneficial for someone or something because it raises awareness and generates interest

examples.x: The fashion designer's latest collection was ridiculed by some critics, but the designer remained optimistic - after all, there is no such thing as bad publicity.

word_list.word: throw dirt enough, and (hopefully|) some will stick
word_list.pronunciation: őł…Ļňąo ä dňą…úňźt …™nňą Ćf √¶nd hňąo äpf…ôli sňĆ Ćm w…™l stňą…™k

senses.definition: used to suggest that if someone makes enough false or negative accusations against another person, some of those accusations are likely to be believed, regardless of whether they are true or false
